children.name: Jacana
children.description: A genus of two tropical jacana species found in the Americas, known for their elaborate facial wattles and frontal shields. Like all jacanas, they have extraordinarily long toes and claws that allow them to walk across floating vegetation, a behaviour that earned them the colloquial name 'lily-trotters'.

children.name: Jacana spinosa
children.description: Found from coastal Mexico south to Panama, this chestnut-and-black jacana sports a bright yellow bill and frontal shield. Highly territorial, females defend large harems of males, each of whom incubates eggs in floating nests built on lily pads.

children.name: Jacana jacana
children.description: A widespread South American jacana with a striking red frontal shield and fleshy red wattles at the base of the bill. It strides confidently across water lily mats in tropical wetlands, foraging for insects and seeds with its remarkably elongated toes.

children.name: Actophilornis
children.description: A genus of two African jacanas distinguished by a bright blue bill and frontal shield, with rich chestnut plumage and white underparts. They inhabit freshwater wetlands across the continent and on Madagascar, employing the same reverse sex-role polyandry as other jacanas.

children.name: Actophilornis africanus
children.description: Easily recognised by its bright blue bill, chestnut back, and pale blue frontal shield extending like a crown across the forehead. Its extraordinarily long toes distribute its weight so effectively that it can run across floating vegetation without sinking.

children.name: Actophilornis albinucha
children.description: Endemic to the freshwater lakes and marshes of western Madagascar, with a white nape and hindneck that distinguish it from the mainland African jacana. Threatened by habitat loss and invasive water hyacinth, its population is declining.

children.name: Irediparra
children.description: A monotypic genus of the Australasian region, with a distinctive bright red fleshy comb-like wattle covering the forehead and crown. The comb-crested jacana is the only jacana found in both Australia and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Irediparra gallinacea
children.description: The sole jacana of Australia and New Guinea, named for the vivid red fleshy comb that sits atop the forehead like a rooster's comb. It inhabits tropical freshwater wetlands with abundant lily pads, where it picks insects from the surface with delicate precision.

children.name: Hydrophasianus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pheasant-tailed jacana, the largest and most elegant member of the family. Unlike other jacanas, this species undergoes a dramatic seasonal plumage change and is a long-distance migratory bird across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Hydrophasianus chirurgus
children.description: The most striking jacana in breeding plumage, with a white head and neck, golden breast, and an extravagantly long black tail that curves like a pheasant's. In winter it becomes far more subdued, with a brown back and reduced tail, and migrates south within its range.

children.name: Microparra
children.description: A monotypic genus of African jacana, the smallest member of the family. The Lesser Jacana is a dainty, delicate bird with a much shorter bill and toes than other jacanas, reflecting its more terrestrial habit and preference for densely vegetated marshes.

children.name: Microparra capensis
children.description: The smallest jacana, found in freshwater wetlands of sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. It has golden-brown upperparts, white underparts, and a chestnut forecrown. Unlike other jacanas, it has relatively short toes and spends less time walking on lily pads, instead preferring to forage in shallow water among emergent vegetation.

children.name: Metopidius
children.description: A monotypic genus of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, characterised by a bronze-olive back and a prominent white supercilium. The bronze-winged jacana is a common resident of lowland wetlands from Pakistan to Vietnam.

children.name: Metopidius indicus
children.description: A handsome jacana with bronze-green upperparts, a broad white eyebrow stripe, and a bright yellow bill shield. It skulks through dense floating vegetation in slow-moving waters, using its enormous feet to grip plant stems with remarkable dexterity.
